attributes.stem: A 54-year-old man is referred by his general practitioner to the pain clinic with the complaint of ongoing pain in his right wrist following a traumatic amputation of the right upper arm at the level of the humerus 3 months previous. He says the pain began within the first few days following the amputation and has persisted since. He describes a burning sensation with associated pins and needles, which has been waking him at night and significantly interfering with his sleep. His history is significant for hypertension and depression managed with Desvenlafaxine, which has states has worsened with the ongoing pain.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate choice of therapy in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
The patient within the above stem is suffering from neuropathic pain secondary to post-amputation limb pain, which is phantom limb pain. Phantom limb pain describes pain experienced in the missing part of the limb with the proposed pathophysiology being that when amputated, many of the cut nerve endings outgrow abnormally and/or become inflamed and send anomalous signals to the brain which are interpreted as pain. The patient described above is experiencing "phantom-limb" neuropathic pain and thus a gabapentinoid such as Gabapentin is the most appropriate choice of therapy.

Research has additionally shown that Calcitonin can reduce the effect of phantom limb pain in the acute (<7 days post amputation) but not the chronic setting.

Gabapentinoids are anti-epileptic medications which is additionally recommended as a first line agent for neuropathic pain. Gabapentin for example, acts via a specific subtype of voltage gated calcium channel where when it binds it alters the normal pain attenuation pathway. The binding to this subunits inhibits nerve injury-induced propagation of within the dorsal root ganglion and dorsal horn neurons. Gabapentin has also been proven to interact with NMDA receptors as well as reducing protein kinase C and inflammatory cytokines. There is some evidence to suggest that gabapentin also stimulates noradrenaline mediated descending inhibition within the supraspinal region, which aids in anti-hypersensitivity action in neuropathic pain. It should be noted that Pregabalin is available on the PBS however Gabapentin is not.

It is important to acknowledge that many pain physicians may institute a tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) or serotonin and noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) as first line management for a patient suffering from neuropathic pain. However, as the patient in the stem is already on Desvenalfaxine it may not be appropriate in this instance.

Opioid therapy is fourth line in the management of neuropathic pain and thus would not be considered an appropriate initial treatment option.

Psychotherapies have been proven as adjunct therapy for persisting phantom-limb pain following medication therapy. Specific adjuncts include mirror visual feedback, desensitisation and reprocessing eye movements, imagery and hypnosis.

Paracetamol would be inappropriate for the management of neuropathic pain.

Neurosurgical review would not be indicated in this situation as there are no current means of first line surgical intervention which could improve symptoms.
learningPoints: Phantom-limb pain is a common occurrence following amputation
First line medication treatment for phantom limb pain is a gabapentinoid or tricyclic/SNRI antidepressant
references: Finnerup NB, Attal N, Haroutounian S, McNicol E, Baron R, Dworkin RH, Gilron I, Haanpää M, Hansson P, Jensen TS, Kamerman PR, Lund K, Moore A, Raja SN, Rice AS, Rowbotham M, Sena E, Siddall P, Smith BH, Wallace M. Pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain in adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Lancet Neurol. 2015 Feb;14(2):162-73. doi: 10.1016/S1474-4422(14)70251-0. Epub 2015 Jan 7. PMID: 25575710; PMCID: PMC4493167. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25575710/

Faculty of Pain Medicine, Australian and New Zealand College of Anaesthetists. National Strategy for Health Practitioner Pain Management Education. 2023. [Internet]. Accessed 9 Jan 2025. Available from: https://www.health.gov.au/sites/default/files/2023-10/national-strategy-for-health-practitioner-pain-management-education.pdf
Schug SA, Palmer GM, Scott DA, et al. Acute Pain Management Scientific Evidence 5th edition. 2020. Available from https://www.anzca.edu.au/safety-advocacy/advocacy/college-publications

Kukkar A, Bali A, Singh N, Jaggi AS. Implications and mechanism of action of gabapentin in neuropathic pain. Arch Pharm Res. 2013 Mar;36(3):237-51. doi: 10.1007/s12272-013-0057-y. Epub 2013 Feb 24. PMID: 23435945. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23435945/
